sections.title: CS4303-P2
sections.text:
University of St Andrews
School of Computer Science
CS4303 1
CS4303 - Videogames
Assignment: Practical 2: Artificial Intelligence
Credits: 30% of the coursework component and the module overall.
Deadline: 9pm 27 March 2026
(NB MMS is the definitive source for deadlines and weights)
Aim / Learning objectives
In this practical assignment, you will develop a video game using the Processing API,
creating an interactive game. The main learning objectives are:
- Implement fundamental AI principles in a game environment, including concepts like
character behaviours and path finding
- Apply common video game architecture patterns that will help you keep
maintainable code
All of this will be done via the Processing framework.
Submission
The deliverables consist of a single zip file containing three elements:
1- A PDF report with an advisory limit of 2000 words, the contents of which are specified
below.
2- A player’s guide for your game, explaining how to play it.
3- The source code for the game you will implement, including:
o A README file explaining how to compile and run your submission.
o Either:
§ A folder containing a processing project (one or more .pde files). The
entry point file should have the same name as the folder.
§ A folder containing: one or more Java files, the Processing JAR files,
and a simple Makefile with two targets. One to compile your
submission and one to run it from the command line.
Do not submit source code that requires a particular IDE (like IntelliJ) or a build system
(like Maven) to be run.

CS4402 2
Problem Specification
Implementation must be done exclusively using the Processing framework and its audio
libraries. The use of any other external libraries, frameworks, or third-party tools is not
permitted. You may also use external assets (graphics, sound effects, music, etc.) in your
game, provided that you clearly cite their sources and ensure compliance with their
respective licenses.
These requirements build upon each other progressively. While correctly completing all four
levels would demonstrate a very good understanding, a well-implemented solution up to
level 2 or 3 is sufficient for a good submission. Focus on ensuring each level is working
correctly before moving to the next one.
Level 1 – Steering Behaviours
Implement character AI in your game. More concretely, you should implement movement
algorithms that allow characters to navigate their environment. Consider using methods
such as the kinematic and steering behaviours as seen in the lectures.
Level 2 – Compound Behaviours
Combine multiple steering behaviours from Level 1 to create compound behaviours such as
flocking (which combines alignment, cohesion, and separation) or obstacle avoidance with
pursuit.
Level 3 – Decision Making
Implement decision-making systems using either decision trees or finite state machines.
Your characters should make decisions based on environmental conditions (e.g., threat
level, resources) and internal states (e.g., health, ammunition). Your implementation should
demonstrate clean code organization, appropriate design patterns, and clear
documentation.
Level 4 – Path Finding
Implement path-finding capabilities for the game characters with A* or similar pathfinding
algorithms. Your implementation should consider quantization and localization. That is, you
should translate between the geometry of the map and the discrete representation where
pathfinding takes place. You might want to integrate with the decision-making system from
Level 3 to create realistic navigation behaviour (e.g., characters choosing different paths
based on their current state or capabilities)
Game Idea/Theme
For this module practicals, you'll be implementing different game systems like physics, AI or
procedural content generation. We aim to give you flexibility in how you approach this: you
can make separate “vertical slices” of games that each show off the required features, work
on a single game throughout the course and keep building on it, or even a mix and match.
For example, you could start with one game and extend it for the second practical, then

CS4402 3
create a separate game for the third, or vice versa. It's entirely up to you how you want to
combine or separate these implementations.
If you're unsure whether your idea would work or how to adapt it to meet the
requirements, please don't hesitate to get in touch with us to discuss it. On the other hand,
if you're struggling to come up with ideas, don't worry - some example themes and concepts
you can use as inspiration follows:
- Consider for example a game like Pacman (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pac-Man)
but in a continuous space. You could adapt it to give ghosts different personalities
and capabilities
- A stealth game, such as 005 (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/005) or Beyond Castle
Wolfestein (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beyond_Castle_Wolfenstein) might be a
good scaffolding to showcase complex AI behaviours
- A dungeon crawler such as Gauntlet
(https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gauntlet_(1985_video_game)) is an excellent
example of multiple enemy types with distinct behaviours.
Remember that the main aim of the practical is for you to familiarize yourself with how AI is
implemented in games. This practical does not expect you to develop a full game with
complex features, but for you to focus on understanding and implementing the core AI
principles.
Report
The PDF report will value conciseness, technical accuracy and informativeness. Given the
visual nature of videogames and the advisory word limit, consider adding images or
diagrams to illustrate your explanations and using references to keep the report concise.
The required sections of the report are:
- An overview and design section, explaining the aim, the core game mechanics built
and how they interact with each other.
- A technical description section explaining the most significant technical challenges
encountered and how they were resolved, talking about the key algorithms or
methods used for the main game features, highlighting any interesting technical
solutions or optimizations.
- Finally, a conclusion and critical review section discussion, where you should reflect
on your work and discuss both your successes and challenges. Consider what you
would do differently if you were to start again, and what you learned from
overcoming difficulties. This is also a good place to mention any improvements you
would implement if you had more time.
Consider using a references section and appendices if needed.

CS4402 4
Marking
This practical will be marked following the standard mark descriptors as given in the Student
Handbook. There follows further guidance as to what is expected:
• To achieve a mark of 7 or higher: Basic implementation of steering behaviors (Level 1)
demonstrating fundamental character movement such as seek or arrive. The
implementation should be functional and adequately reported.
• To achieve a mark of 11 or higher: In addition to Level 1 requirements, implementation
of compound behaviors (Level 2) showing characters that can combine multiple
steering behaviors effectively (e.g., flocking behavior). The code should be well￾structured and properly reported.
• To achieve a mark of 14 or higher: Building upon Levels 1 and 2, implementation of
decision-making systems (Level 3) using either decision trees or finite state machines.
The AI architecture should demonstrate good software engineering principles with
clear separation of concerns and appropriate design patterns. The implementation
should be well reported
• To achieve a mark of 17: In addition to the requirements for a mark of 14,
implementation of robust pathfinding (Level 4) that effectively handles conversion
between continuous and discrete space and integrates with the decision-making
system. The implementation should take character specifics into consideration. The
practical should be very well reported.
• To achieve a mark greater than 17: In addition to the requirements for a mark of 17,
evidence of an exceptional achievement in terms of technical challenge, together with
an outstanding in-depth and insightful report.
For lateness, Scheme A will be applied, which will subtract 1 mark per 24 hour period, or
part thereof.
Policies and Guidelines
Your attention is drawn to the following:
Marking
See the standard mark descriptors in the School Student Handbook:
http://info.cs.st-andrews.ac.uk/student-handbook/learning￾teaching/feedback.html#Mark_Descriptors
Good academic practice
The University policy on Good Academic Practice applies:
https://www.st-andrews.ac.uk/education/handbook/good-academic-practice/
Lateness
https://info.cs.st-andrews.ac.uk/student-handbook/learning￾teaching/assessment.html#lateness-penalties
